macro_segments.title: Introduction to Mishnah: Worker's Right to Eat and the Dispute Between Rabbi Eliezer ben Chisma and the Sages

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: A worker may eat cucumbers and pressed dates even if worth a dinar, exceeding his wage
Rabbi Eliezer ben Chisma vs. the Sages: The disagreement and the Sages' ethical teaching
Gemara's identification of the actual dispute: Whether the Sages add the instruction not to be gluttonous

macro_segments.title: Rav Assi's Positions: A Worker Can Eat the First Cluster Despite Multiple Interpretations

micro_segments.title: First case: Worker hired for only one cluster may eat the entire cluster
Second case: Worker hired for whole day may eat the first cluster picked
Gemara's explanation: Both cases needed because one involves no alternative for the owner, the other does

macro_segments.title: The Hidden Scroll of Rav Chiya: Issi ben Yehuda's Radical Interpretation and Its Rejection

micro_segments.title: Rav's discovery of a hidden scroll containing Issi ben Yehuda's permissive view on vineyard access
Rav's objection: Issi's position would make farming impossible if anyone could enter and eat freely
Rav Ashi's objection to Rav Kahana: Even the reinterpreted view creates practical chaos
Rav Kahana's reinterpretation: Issi means self-appointed workers can feed themselves while working

macro_segments.title: Conceptual Framework: Is the Worker Eating His Own Salary or Receiving a Heaven-Given Right?

micro_segments.title: The central question: Whether the right to eat is part of the worker's salary or a right from the Torah
Proof from the mishnah: A worker can eat more than his wage, suggesting it is not part of salary
Conclusion: The Torah permits eating beyond salary even if conceptualized as salary

macro_segments.title: Reassignment Test: Can a Worker Assign His Eating Rights to Wife and Children?

micro_segments.title: Case of a worker attempting to reassign eating rights to wife and children instead of eating himself
Halakha: The worker cannot reassign; this proves the right is from heaven, not salary
The special case of a nazir: Rabbinical safeguard keeping a nazir away from vineyards altogether

macro_segments.title: Drying Figs Case: When Gmar Melacha Is Not Yet Complete and Expectations of Benefit

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Worker hired to dry figs may eat because the work is not yet complete
Stipulation allowing son to eat instead: Son is obligated in ma'aser while worker is exempt
Explanation: Worker eating does not look like a sale; son eating does look like a sale

macro_segments.title: Neta Revai Case: The Employer's Obligation to Compensate When Rights Are Withheld

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Worker hired for fourth-year trees unaware that he cannot eat the fruit
Employer's remedy: Redeem the fruit and feed it to the worker to compensate for lost benefit
Gemara's reasoning: Even if the right is from heaven, the appearance of a fraudulent sale must be remedied

macro_segments.title: Wine Barrels Case: Gmar Melacha, Lees Removal, and Reasonable Expectations

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Worker hired to reseal wine barrels without being told lees were already removed
Tanna Kamma and Rabbi Akiva disagreement: When gmar melacha occurs in wine production
Resolution: In the normal work package, sealing and lees-removal go together, so worker had reasonable expectation

macro_segments.title: Negotiating Away Eating Rights: Who Can Do It and Under What Conditions

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: A person can negotiate for himself, his adult wife, and adult children and slaves
Mishnah: A person cannot negotiate for minor children, minor slaves, or animals
Gemara's first explanation: The inability to negotiate minors' rights stems from the father not feeding them
Baraita of Rav Ushaya: Allows negotiation for slaves but not minor children, even when feeding them

macro_segments.title: Debate Over Entitlement to Slave Labor: Impact on Negotiation Rights for Minor Slaves

micro_segments.title: First interpretation: Both tannaim hold eating is salary; they debate whether feeding confers entitlement
Challenge: If eating is salary and master owns slave labor, why can't he negotiate minor slaves?
Resolution: Debated whether a master is entitled to slave labor without feeding the slave

macro_segments.title: Final Framework: Both Views Hold It Is From Heaven; Debate Is Over Negotiability by Adults vs. Minors

micro_segments.title: Common ground: All agree the eating right is from heaven (min hashamayim), not part of salary
Adults can waive the heaven-given right in exchange for higher compensation
Minors cannot waive a heaven-given right; the Torah protects their entitlement
Unresolved: The broader question remains whether eating is mishlo or mishamayim

topical_tags.term: Rabbi Eliezer ben Chisma vs. Sages
Entitlement to slave labor without feeding
Ben da'at (adult with capacity to waive rights)
